Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
Initialize the project schedule by setting the current time (t) to 0.
Identify the set of 'eligible' activities, which are those whose predecessors have all been completed.
Calculate the Earliest Start Time (EST) for each eligible activity based on the project network constraints.
Sort the eligible activities in non-decreasing order of their EST values.
Check the resource availability for the current time (t) against the resource requirements of the sorted eligible activities.
Select activities from the sorted list that can be scheduled without exceeding the available resource capacity.
Update the resource availability by subtracting the requirements of the selected activities.
Assign the selected activities to start at time (t) and calculate their finish times based on their durations.
Move the current time (t) to the next earliest finish time of any activity currently in progress.
Update the set of eligible activities by marking newly completed activities as finished and checking if their successors are now ready.
Repeat the process of selecting, scheduling, and updating until all activities in the project are completed.
Latest Start Time Priority Rule (LST):
Calculate the Latest Start Time (LST) for each activity using the Critical Path Method (CPM) based on the project deadline.
Initialize the project schedule by setting the current time (t) to 0 and identifying the set of eligible activities (those whose predecessors are completed).
At time t, identify the set of activities that are both eligible and have sufficient resource availability to be scheduled.
Sort the eligible activities in non-decreasing order of their LST values; if LST values are tied, apply a secondary tie-breaking rule (e.g., Shortest Processing Time).
Iterate through the sorted list and schedule activities as long as the cumulative resource requirements do not exceed the capacity for each resource type at time t.
Update the set of completed activities and the resource availability profile based on the scheduled activities.
Advance the current time (t) to the next earliest completion time of any currently active activity.
Repeat the process of identifying eligible activities, sorting by LST, and scheduling until all activities are completed.
Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MSL):
Initialize the project schedule by setting the current time t = 0 and identifying all activities with no predecessors (eligible activities).
Calculate the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity using the Critical Path Method (CPM) based on the project deadline.
Calculate the slack for each eligible activity using the formula: Slack = LFT - (Current Time + Remaining Duration).
Identify the set of activities that are currently eligible to start, meaning all their predecessors are completed and resources are available.
Rank the eligible activities in ascending order based on their calculated slack values (the smallest slack value gets the highest priority).
Assign resources to the eligible activities according to the priority ranking until no more activities can be started due to resource constraints or lack of eligible tasks.
Update the current time t to the earliest finish time of the activities currently in progress.
Mark the completed activities as finished and update the set of eligible activities by checking if any new activities have had all their predecessors completed.
Repeat the process of calculating slack, ranking, and scheduling until all activities in the project are completed.
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Initialize the project schedule by setting the current time t = 0 and identifying the set of all activities with no predecessors (eligible activities).
Maintain a set of 'in-progress' activities and a set of 'completed' activities, initially empty.
At each decision point (when an activity finishes or at t=0), identify the set of 'eligible' activities whose predecessors have all been completed.
For each eligible activity, calculate its 'Greatest Resource Demand' (GRD) value, typically defined as the product of the activity's duration and its total resource consumption across all resource types, or the sum of its resource requirements.
Rank all eligible activities in descending order based on their calculated GRD values.
Attempt to schedule the eligible activities in the ranked order, checking if sufficient resource capacity is available for each activity at the current time t.
If an activity can be scheduled without exceeding resource constraints, update the resource availability, move the activity to the 'in-progress' set, and record its start time.
If an activity cannot be scheduled due to resource constraints, leave it in the 'eligible' set and proceed to the next activity in the ranked list.
Advance the current time t to the earliest finish time among all 'in-progress' activities.
Update the 'in-progress' set by moving finished activities to the 'completed' set and releasing their allocated resources.
Repeat the process from step 3 until all activities are moved to the 'completed' set.
Most Successors Priority Rule (MOS):
Initialize the project schedule by identifying all activities, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence constraints.
Calculate the number of successors for each activity by traversing the project network (typically using a directed acyclic graph).
Initialize the set of 'eligible' activities (those whose predecessors have all been completed) and the set of 'in-progress' activities.
At each decision point (when resources become available or an activity finishes), update the set of eligible activities.
Rank all currently eligible activities in descending order based on their number of successors (the MOS priority value).
Select the highest-ranked eligible activity that can be accommodated by the currently available resource capacity.
Schedule the selected activity, update the available resource profile, and move the activity to the 'in-progress' set.
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities are scheduled or no further activities can be started due to resource constraints.
Advance the simulation clock to the next completion time of an in-progress activity and repeat the process until the project is complete.
Least Successors Priority Rule (LES):
Initialize the project by identifying all activities, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence constraints.
Calculate the number of successors for each activity by traversing the precedence graph (the number of activities that depend on the completion of the current activity).
Initialize the set of eligible activities (those whose predecessors have all been completed) and the set of scheduled activities.
At each decision point where resources are available, identify the subset of eligible activities that can be scheduled without violating resource constraints.
Apply the Least Successors (LES) priority rule: among the subset of feasible activities, select the activity with the smallest number of successors to schedule next.
Update the resource availability and the set of completed activities once the selected activity is scheduled.
Update the set of eligible activities by checking if any new activities have had all their predecessors completed.
Repeat the selection and scheduling process until all activities are scheduled and the project is complete.
Shortest Processing Time Priority Rule (:
Initialize the project by identifying all activities, their durations, resource requirements, and precedence constraints.
Define the set of available resources and their capacities over time.
Create an empty set for 'Completed Activities' and a set for 'Eligible Activities' (those whose predecessors are finished).
Maintain a 'Current Time' clock, starting at zero.
At each decision point (when resources become available or an activity finishes), update the 'Eligible Activities' list based on precedence constraints.
Calculate the priority for each activity in the 'Eligible Activities' list using the Shortest Processing Time (SPT) rule, where priority is inversely proportional to activity duration.
Sort the 'Eligible Activities' in ascending order of their durations (shortest duration first).
Iterate through the sorted list and schedule activities that satisfy resource availability constraints at the 'Current Time'.
Update the 'Current Time' to the earliest finish time of any currently running activity.
Move finished activities to the 'Completed Activities' set and release their occupied resources.
Repeat steps 5 through 10 until all activities are moved to the 'Completed Activities' set.
Longest Processing Time Priority Rule (L:
Calculate the total duration of each activity by summing the durations of all its successors in the project network (or use the activity's own duration if the rule is applied to individual tasks).
Initialize the set of eligible activities (those whose predecessors have been completed) and the set of scheduled activities.
At each decision point where a resource becomes available, identify all activities that are currently eligible to start based on precedence constraints.
Filter the eligible activities to include only those that can be accommodated by the currently available resource capacities.
Sort the filtered eligible activities in descending order based on their calculated processing times (or total work content).
Select the activity with the longest processing time from the sorted list.
Assign the selected activity to the available resources and update the resource availability profile.
Update the set of eligible activities by checking if any new activities have had all their predecessors completed due to the scheduling of the current activity.
Repeat the process from step 3 until all activities in the project have been scheduled.
Critical Path Method Priority Rule (CPM):
Calculate the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity using a backward pass calculation, assuming infinite resource availability.
Define the priority value for each activity as its LFT, where smaller LFT values indicate higher priority.
Initialize the schedule by setting the current time (t) to 0 and identifying the set of eligible activities (those whose predecessors are completed).
At time t, sort the set of eligible activities in ascending order based on their LFT values.
Iteratively select activities from the sorted list and schedule them if sufficient resources are available for the duration of the activity.
Update the available resource profile for the duration of the scheduled activities.
Advance the current time (t) to the next earliest completion time of any active activity.
Update the set of eligible activities by checking if any new activities have had all their predecessors completed.
Repeat the scheduling, resource checking, and time advancement steps until all activities are completed.
Resource-Constrained Critical Path Prior:
Calculate the Latest Finish Time (LFT) for each activity using the standard Critical Path Method (CPM) based on project duration and finish-to-start dependencies.
Define the priority rule for the scheduling process, which is to rank activities in non-decreasing order of their LFT values (the activity with the smallest LFT has the highest priority).
Initialize the schedule by setting the current time (t) to 0 and identifying the set of 'eligible' activities (those whose predecessors have all been completed).
Maintain a set of 'in-progress' activities and track the remaining resource availability for each resource type at time t.
At time t, sort all currently eligible activities based on their LFT values.
Iteratively select the highest-priority eligible activity and check if sufficient resources are available to start it without exceeding capacity.
If resources are sufficient, schedule the activity to start at time t, update the remaining resource capacities, and move the activity to the 'in-progress' set.
If resources are insufficient for the highest-priority activity, skip it and attempt to schedule the next highest-priority activity from the eligible set.
Once no more eligible activities can be started at time t, advance the clock (t) to the earliest completion time of any activity currently in the 'in-progress' set.
Update the set of eligible activities by adding any activities whose predecessors have finished at the new time t.
Repeat the process of scheduling and advancing time until all activities are completed.
